g.data-narration: Welcome to Branch and Bound - an intelligent algorithmic paradigm for solving complex optimization problems efficiently.
Branch and Bound systematically explores the solution space by branching into subproblems and pruning branches that cannot yield optimal solutions.
g.text: An algorithmic paradigm that solves combinatorial optimization problems by
• Computing bounds to estimate best possible solution in each branch

g.data-narration: Three essential components work together: branching divides problems, bounding estimates solutions, and search strategies explore the tree.
The state space tree represents all possible solutions - nodes are partial solutions, leaves are complete solutions we evaluate.
Pruning eliminates unpromising branches - if a bound indicates no better solution exists, we discard that entire subtree.
Three search strategies explore the tree differently: FIFO levels the tree, LIFO goes deep first, Best-First prioritizes promising nodes.
The 0/1 Knapsack problem: maximize profit with weight limit. We use fractional knapsack relaxation as our bounding function.
g.text: Given n items with profits p₁,p₂,...,pn and weights w₁,w₂,...,wn,
capacity W. Find maximum profit without exceeding capacity.
Subject to Σ(wᵢ × xᵢ) ≤ W, xᵢ ∈ {0,1}

g.data-narration: The upper bound uses fractional knapsack - sort items by profit/weight ratio, take whole items, then fraction of next item.
g.text: Upper Bound = Current Profit + (Remaining Capacity × Max Profit/Weight of Remaining Items)

g.data-narration: Example: n=4 items with profits [10,10,12,18], weights [2,4,6,9], capacity=15. First compute profit/weight ratios.
The Traveling Salesman Problem asks for the shortest Hamiltonian cycle. Branch and Bound uses reduced cost matrices for bounding.
g.text: Find the shortest Hamiltonian cycle visiting all cities exactly once.

g.data-narration: For TSP, we compute lower bounds using reduced cost matrices - subtract minimum value in each row and column.
g.text: Step 1: Subtract minimum value in each row from all elements in that row
Step 2: Subtract minimum value in each column from all elements in that column
Step 3: Lower Bound = Sum of all reduction constants + path cost so far

g.data-narration: Consider a 4-city TSP. The reduced cost matrix provides optimistic lower bounds that help prune expensive routes early.
Best-First Search typically finds optimal solutions fastest by always exploring the most promising node based on bound values.
Branch and Bound dramatically reduces search space through intelligent pruning, making NP-hard problems tractable in practice.
g.text: Why Branch and Bound is Efficient
Worst-case remains exponential, but pruning eliminates
large portions of search tree in practice

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: three components (branching, bounding, search), two applications (Knapsack, TSP), and optimality conditions.
g.text: • Three Components: Branching + Bounding + Search Strategy
• Knapsack: Upper bound via fractional relaxation (sorted by P/W)
• TSP: Lower bound via reduced cost matrix (rows then columns)
• Best-First: Most efficient - explores nodes with best bounds first

g.data-narration: Branch and Bound combines systematic enumeration with intelligent pruning to solve optimization problems efficiently in real applications.
g.text: Summary: Branch and Bound in Practice
Used in logistics, scheduling, network design, and resource allocation.
The key to efficiency lies in designing tight, computable bounding functions.
